text: Low energy availability (inadequate energy intake relative to exercise energy expenditure) causes a syndrome of deleterious health and performance outcomes in both female and male athletes, termed Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (REDs).
Low carbohydrate availability, not just total energy deficiency, plays a growing role in the development of REDs signs and symptoms.
There is a bidirectional interplay between mental health and REDs, where low energy availability can impair mental health and mental health issues can contribute to low energy availability.
Male athletes are also susceptible to the negative health and performance effects of low energy availability, not only female athletes as previously emphasized.
The impact of low energy availability depends on whether exposure is problematic or adaptable, moderated by individual factors such as duration, magnitude, and individual physiology.

Clinical diagnosis of REDs should use severity and risk stratification (via the REDs Clinical Assessment Tool) to guide training and competition recommendations, ranging from full clearance to restricted participation.
Body composition assessments in athletes should follow safe and effective guidelines to help prevent triggering or worsening REDs.
